Name: RateDistortionForAdaptivePruning
Title: Adaptive Pruning for Model Compression via Rate-Distortion Theory
Short Hypothesis: Can we develop an adaptive pruning method that dynamically allocates bits to model parameters based on their task-relevant distortion, achieving a better rate-distortion tradeoff compared to static pruning methods?
Related Work: Recent works such as Isik et al. (2021) and Bu et al. (2019) have explored the use of rate-distortion theory for model pruning and compression. These studies primarily focus on static pruning methods and derive theoretical bounds for the tradeoff between compression rate and distortion. However, they do not address the dynamic allocation of bits to parameters based on their task-relevance. This proposal aims to extend these works by developing an adaptive pruning method that considers the dynamic nature of model parameters and their impact on task performance.
Abstract: We propose an adaptive pruning method for model compression using rate-distortion theory. Unlike static pruning methods, our approach dynamically allocates bits to model parameters based on their task-relevant distortion. By treating model compression as a lossy source coding problem, we derive a rate-distortion tradeoff that balances the compression rate with the distortion in task performance. We develop an adaptive pruning algorithm that iteratively updates the bit allocation to minimize the task-relevant distortion while maintaining a target compression rate. Our method is validated through experiments on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet datasets, showing superior performance in terms of the rate-distortion tradeoff compared to existing static pruning methods.
Experiments: Derive the rate-distortion function for a neural network model, considering the task-relevant distortion metric.
Develop an adaptive pruning algorithm that dynamically updates the bit allocation for model parameters based on their task-relevant distortion.
Implement the adaptive pruning algorithm on a neural network trained on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet datasets.
Compare the rate-distortion tradeoff of the adaptive pruning method with static pruning methods, such as magnitude-based pruning and Hessian-weighted K-means clustering.
Evaluate the performance of the compressed models on the test sets of CIFAR-10 and ImageNet, using metrics such as accuracy, compression rate, and task-relevant distortion.
Conduct ablation studies to analyze the impact of different distortion metrics and bit allocation strategies on the performance of the adaptive pruning method.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The adaptive pruning algorithm may require more computational resources and time compared to static methods.
The choice of task-relevant distortion metric can significantly affect the performance of the adaptive pruning method.
The generalization of the adaptive pruning method to different model architectures and datasets may require further tuning and validation.
The theoretical bounds derived for the rate-distortion tradeoff may not always be tight, leading to suboptimal pruning decisions.
